labeledSubSubTopics.title: 6.4D Stratospheric ozone depletion and ODSs (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (b) asks for a man-made factor (ozone-depleting substances such as CFCs/halons) driving stratospheric ozone depletion.

parts.content: Describe the relationship between ozone levels and the UV radiation index.
parts.markscheme:
- As ozone levels decrease, UV radiation index increases $\implies$ inverse relationship <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Higher UV radiation index means more harmful UV rays reach the Earth's surface <marks>1 mark</marks>

<marks>2 marks max</marks>

parts.content: State one man-made factor that affects ozone levels in the stratosphere.
parts.markscheme:
The command term 'state' means students must give a specific name, value or other brief answer without explanation or calculation.

Award <marks>1 mark max</marks> for any of the following marking points:

- ODSs (ozone depleting substances)
- CFCs
- halons
- methylbromide
- carbon tetrachloride

<note>ODS released into the environment can destroy stratospheric ozone. ODS can include chemicals used in aerosols, pesticides, refrigerants, and fire retardants.</note>

parts.content: Evaluate the role of the Montreal Protocol in the protection of stratospheric ozone.
parts.markscheme:
- The Montreal Protocol was an international agreement to phase out the production and use of ozone-depleting substances (ODSs) such as CFCs and halons <marks>1 mark</marks>
- It led to a decrease in the levels of ODSs in the atmosphere, allowing the ozone layer to start recovering <marks>1 mark</marks>

$$
\text{Recovery of the ozone layer} \implies \text{reduced UV radiation reaching the Earth's surface}
$$

<marks>1 mark</marks>

- The Montreal Protocol is considered one of the most successful international environmental agreements <marks>1 mark</marks>
- However, it does not address other greenhouse gases that contribute to climate change, which can also affect the ozone layer <marks>1 mark</marks>

<marks>5 marks max</marks>

questions.specification:
The table below shows the average total column ozone levels (measured in Dobson Units, DU) at two different monitoring stations between 1970 and 2010.

| Year | Halley Bay, Antarctica (75°S) [DU] | Arosa, Switzerland (47°N) [DU] |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1970 | 300 | 335 |
| 1990 | 180 | 315 |
| 2010 | 144 | 310 |

labeledSubSubTopics.title: 6.4C The Montreal protocol

labeledSubSubTopics.title: 6.4A UV radiation
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (a) focuses on negative impacts of increased UV radiation on terrestrial plant life, linking ozone depletion to UV effects.

parts.content: Using the data provided for Halley Bay, calculate the percentage decrease in ozone levels between 1970 and 1990.
parts.markscheme:
The command term "calculate" requires a numerical answer and the showing of working.

- Change in DU: $$300 - 180 = 120$$ DU <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Percentage change: $$\frac{120}{300} \times 100 = 40\%$$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: State one negative impact on terrestrial plant life caused by the increased levels of UV radiation resulting from ozone depletion.
parts.markscheme:
The command term "state" requires a brief answer without explanation.

Award <marks>1 mark max</marks> for any of the following:
- Reduced rates of photosynthesis.
- Damage to leaf tissues/DNA damage in plant cells.
- Reduced crop yields/stunted growth.
- Inhibition of seedling development.

parts.content: Explain the chemical process by which halogenated organic gases, such as CFCs, deplete the stratospheric ozone layer.
parts.markscheme:
The command term "explain" requires a detailed account including reasons/causes.

Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for each relevant point, up to <marks>3 marks max</marks>:
- UV radiation hits the CFC molecule, causing a chlorine atom to break away.
- The free chlorine atom reacts with an ozone molecule ($$O_3$$), breaking it apart to form oxygen ($$O_2$$) and chlorine monoxide ($$ClO$$).
- The chlorine atom is subsequently reformed/released during further reactions, allowing it to act as a catalyst and destroy many more ozone molecules in a repetitive cycle.

parts.content: Describe how the Montreal Protocol has acted as an effective international management strategy for protecting the ozone layer.
parts.markscheme:
The command term "describe" requires a detailed account of the strategy.

Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for each relevant point, up to <marks>3 marks max</marks>:
- It created a legally binding timeline for the phase-out of production and consumption of ozone-depleting substances (ODS).
- It included a "Multilateral Fund" to provide financial and technical assistance to developing nations to help them transition away from ODS.
- The treaty is dynamic/flexible, allowing for adjustments and amendments as new scientific evidence or technological alternatives (like HFCs) emerge.

labeledSubSubTopics.title: 4.4A Water pollution

parts.content: Compare the characteristics of point source pollution and non-point source pollution, providing a named example for each.
Using the pollution management model, evaluate strategies to manage the impact of non-biodegradable plastic waste in marine ecosystems.
parts.markscheme:
**Altering human activity (Replace)**
- Educational campaigns to discourage single-use plastics <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Legislation/taxes on plastic bags or straws to reduce consumption <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Development of biodegradable alternatives/bioplastics <marks>1 mark</marks>
- _Evaluation_: Addresses the root cause but requires significant cultural shift and may be slow to show results <marks>1 mark</marks>

**Controlling release at source (Regulate)**
- Improving municipal waste management/recycling infrastructure to prevent leakage into waterways <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Implementing circular economy models where manufacturers are responsible for plastic recovery <marks>1 mark</marks>
- _Evaluation_: Effective in urban areas but expensive for LEDCs to implement and does not address plastics already in the system <marks>1 mark</marks>

**Clean-up and Restoration (Remediate)**
- Large-scale ocean cleanup projects (e.g., using booms to collect surface plastic) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Community-led beach cleaning initiatives <marks>1 mark</marks>
- _Evaluation_: Highly visible and removes immediate threats to wildlife, but is the most expensive/least efficient and does not stop the flow of new pollutants <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note> Award 7 marks max. To achieve full marks, at least one strategy from each level of the model must be evaluated.

parts.content: “The success of pollution management is determined more by cultural and economic factors than by the availability of technological solutions.” To what extent do you agree with this statement?
parts.markscheme:
_Refer to paper 2 markbands._

_Answers may include:_

*   **Understanding concepts and terminology**: pollution management model (replace, regulate, restore); Environmental Value Systems (EVS) (technocentric, anthropocentric, ecocentric); circular economy; sustainability; tragedy of the commons; LEDCs vs MEDCs; persistent vs biodegradable pollutants.
*   **Breadth in addressing and linking**: how economic development (GDP) influences a country's priority (Environment vs Industry); how cultural attitudes (e.g., consumerism vs minimalism) drive waste production; the role of technology in monitoring (satellites) and remediation (renewables).
*   **Examples**: contrast between recycling success in Northern Europe (cultural/economic) vs plastic pollution in rapidly industrializing nations; the use of DDT (economic trade-off between malaria and environment); the transition to electric vehicles (technological availability vs economic subsidies/infrastructure).
*   **Balanced analysis**: 
    *   _Support for statement_: even where technology exists (e.g., carbon capture, advanced recycling), high costs may prevent adoption in LEDCs. Cultural resistance to changing diets or transport habits can stall management regardless of tech.
    *   _Counter-argument_: without technology (e.g., scrubbers, renewable energy tech, GPS monitoring), even a willing culture cannot effectively reduce certain pollutants. Technology can drive down costs, making management economically feasible over time.
*   **Conclusion**: Should summarize the relative importance of both. For example, technology provides the tools, but cultural and economic frameworks provide the will and the means to use them. In many cases, the most effective management occurs when economic incentives (taxes/subsidies) align with cultural values and technological capability.

labeledSubSubTopics.title: 6.4B Ozone depletion
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Outlining the steady state of ozone concentration involves understanding the natural balance of ozone formation and destruction (the Chapman cycle), which is foundational to the study of ozone depletion in topic 6.4B.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 1: Foundation  > 1.2 Systems > 1.2A Systems
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 1.2A Systems
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The term 'steady state' is a specific systems concept involving equilibrium, which is introduced and defined in topic 1.2A.

parts.content: Outline the role of stratospheric ozone in protecting life on Earth from UV radiation and describe how a steady-state ozone concentration is maintained in the stratosphere.
parts.markscheme:
- Stratospheric ozone absorbs a large proportion of incoming ultraviolet radiation, especially UVB; reducing the amount reaching Earth’s surface <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Reduced UV exposure lowers biological damage (e.g., reduced DNA damage/mutation, skin cancer risk, cataracts; impacts on plants/phytoplankton) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Ozone formation: UV photolysis of oxygen, $\mathrm{O_2 + UV \rightarrow 2O}$; followed by $\mathrm{O + O_2 \rightarrow O_3}$ (often with a third body, $\mathrm{M}$) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Ozone destruction: photolysis and recombination, e.g. $\mathrm{O_3 + UV \rightarrow O_2 + O}$ and/or $\mathrm{O + O_3 \rightarrow 2O_2}$; steady state occurs when rate of production balances rate of loss under natural conditions <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain how human activities can disrupt the natural steady state of stratospheric ozone and evaluate the effectiveness of one international response.
parts.markscheme:
- Human release of ozone-depleting substances (ODS) such as CFCs/halons; they are stable in the troposphere and reach the stratosphere <marks>1 mark</marks>
- UV breaks ODS releasing halogen radicals (e.g., $\mathrm{Cl\cdot}$); radicals catalyse ozone destruction (e.g., $\mathrm{Cl\cdot + O_3 \rightarrow ClO\cdot + O_2}$; $\mathrm{ClO\cdot + O \rightarrow Cl\cdot + O_2}$) so one radical can destroy many $\mathrm{O_3}$ molecules <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Net effect is reduced ozone concentration/“ozone hole”, increasing surface UVB with impacts on human health and ecosystems <marks>1 mark</marks>
- International response identified (e.g., Montreal Protocol); aims to phase out production/consumption of ODS <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Effectiveness: evidence of reduced atmospheric concentrations of key ODS and signs of gradual ozone recovery (long time lag due to persistence of ODS) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Limitations/issues: uneven compliance/illegal production; substitute chemicals may have other impacts (e.g., some substitutes are greenhouse gases); recovery is slow and depends on continued enforcement <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Supported conclusion/value judgement on overall effectiveness (e.g., generally effective due to widespread ratification and measurable declines in ODS, but long recovery time and enforcement/substitution issues remain) <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: To what extent should the management of stratospheric ozone depletion be prioritised compared with other global atmospheric issues?
parts.markscheme:
0 marks: The response is not relevant or does not reach a standard described by the descriptors below.

1–3 marks: Minimal knowledge of stratospheric ozone depletion and/or other atmospheric issues. Statements are largely unlinked or descriptive with little comparison. Limited or no use of ESS terminology. Few or no examples. Conclusion absent or unsupported.

4–6 marks: Some sound knowledge and understanding of ozone depletion and at least one other atmospheric issue (e.g., climate change, urban air pollution). Some relevant ESS terminology and some comparison of severity, scale, time lag, reversibility, and management feasibility. Uses at least one relevant example (e.g., Montreal Protocol) with limited explanation. Conclusion present but may be limited in support.

7–9 marks: Substantial, well-linked knowledge and understanding. Balanced, comparative analysis of prioritisation using multiple criteria (e.g., magnitude of impacts, urgency, reversibility, uncertainty, equity, cost-effectiveness, co-benefits/trade-offs). Integrates pertinent examples (e.g., Montreal Protocol success; interactions between ozone and climate via ODS/HCFCs/HFCs). For HL responses, includes awareness of different lenses (ecological, economic, cultural/political) in evaluating priorities. Clear, well-supported judgement on the extent to which ozone management should be prioritised relative to other issues.

labeledSubSubTopics.title: 8.3B Acid rain
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (a) asks for impacts of acid rain; part (c) focuses on managing sulphur dioxide effects on ecosystems—core content of the Acid rain subtopic within urban air pollution.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 8: Human populations and urban systems > 8.3 Urban air pollution > 8.3A Urban air pollution
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 8.3A Urban air pollution
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Parts (b) and (c) involve classifying pollutants (primary vs secondary) and evaluating pollution management strategies, which are broader urban air pollution concepts beyond acid rain alone.

parts.content: Outline four impacts of acid rain on the natural and urban environment.
parts.markscheme:
Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for any of the following, <marks>4 marks max</marks>:

- Acid rain dissolves nutrients from the soil.

- Acid rain leaches aluminium from the soil; aluminium is toxic for plants and animals.

-  If aluminium reaches bodies of water, it will kill fish since it affects the gills of fish.

- Acid rain slows the growth of plants/reduces primary productivity.

- Acid rain damages the leaves of plants/trees affecting photosynthesis rate.

- Acid rain is toxic/kills phytoplankton, insects; it also affects animal exoskeletons.

- Acid rain damages/corrodes limestone and other construction materials.

-  Acid rain damages buildings and sculptures.

<note>Accept any other reasonable answers.</note>

parts.content: Using named examples where relevant, distinguish between primary and secondary pollutants.
parts.markscheme:
### Primary pollutants
- <marks>1 mark</marks> Primary pollutants are emitted directly into the atmosphere from a source.
- Examples:
  - <marks>1 mark</marks> Carbon monoxide (CO) from vehicle exhaust.
  - <marks>1 mark</marks> Sulfur dioxide ($SO_2$) from power plants burning fossil fuels.
  - <marks>1 mark</marks> Particulate matter from construction sites or unpaved roads.

### Secondary pollutants
- <marks>1 mark</marks> Secondary pollutants are formed in the atmosphere through chemical reactions involving primary pollutants and other atmospheric compounds.
- Examples:
  - <marks>1 mark</marks> Ozone ($O_3$) formed from the reaction of nitrogen oxides ($NO_x$) and volatile organic compounds (VOCs) in the presence of sunlight.

$$
NO + VOCs + \text{sunlight} \rightarrow NO_2 + O_3
$$

  - <marks>1 mark</marks> Acid rain formed from the reaction of sulfur dioxide ($SO_2$) and nitrogen oxides ($NO_x$) with water vapor in the atmosphere, producing sulfuric acid ($H_2SO_4$) and nitric acid ($HNO_3$).

<marks>7 marks max</marks>

parts.content: Evaluate how pollution management strategies may regulate, reduce, and repair the impact of sulphur dioxide on ecosystems.
parts.markscheme:
The command term "evaluate" means students must make an appraisal by weighing up the strengths and limitations.

Answers may demonstrate:

- Understanding concepts & terminology: pollution, urban air pollution, pollution strategies, altering human activities strategies, reducing/controlling the pollutant at the point of release strategies, restoring ecosystems strategies, combustion of fossil fuels, sulphur dioxide, acid rain, primary and secondary pollutants, renewable energy sources, scrubbers in power stations, catalytic converters, adding limestone to lakes, low emission vehicles, preventative strategies including altering human behaviour or controlling the release of pollutants, restorative strategies like clean-up and restoration of damaged ecosystems, etc.

- Breadth in addressing and linking strategies to advantages and disadvantages, preventative strategies including altering human behaviour or controlling the release of pollutants, restorative strategies like clean-up and restoration of damaged ecosystems.

- Examples of pollution management strategies, for example, altering human activity strategy can be policies and incentives, stopping fossil fuels or increasing the use of renewable energy sources, scrubbers in power stations, catalytic converters, low emission vehicles, restorative strategies like adding limestone to lakes, etc.

- Balanced analysis evaluating the extent to which different strategies might help reduce the impact of $SO_2$, the advantages and disadvantages of the mentioned strategies.

- A conclusion that is consistent with, and supported by analysis and examples given, for example, although switching to renewable energy sources requires significant investment from governments and acceptance from the population, it can decrease the production of sulphur dioxide, which has a detrimental effect on ecosystems and societies.

***Use the following markband to allocate marks:***

<marks>0 marks</marks>:

The response does not reach a standard described by the descriptors below and is not relevant to the question.

<marks>1–3 marks</marks>:
The response contains:

- Minimal evidence of knowledge and understanding of ESS issues and concepts
- Fragmented knowledge statements that are poorly linked to the context of the question
- Some appropriate use of ESS terminology
- No examples, where required, or examples with insufficient explanation/relevance
- Superficial analysis that amounts to no more than a list of facts/ideas
- Judgements/conclusions that are vague or not supported by evidence/argument.

<marks>4–6 marks</marks>:
The response contains:

- Some evidence of sound knowledge and understanding of ESS issues and concepts
- Knowledge statements that are effectively linked to the context of the question
- Largely appropriate use of ESS terminology
- Some use of relevant examples, where required, but with limited explanation
- Clear analysis that shows a degree of balance
- Some clear judgements/conclusions that are supported by limited evidence/arguments.

<marks>7–9 marks</marks>:
The response contains:

- Substantial evidence of sound knowledge and understanding of ESS issues and concepts
- A wide breadth of knowledge statements that are effectively linked to each other and to the context of the question
- Consistent, appropriate and precise use of ESS terminology
- Effective use of pertinent, well-explained examples, where required, showing some originality
- Thorough, well-balanced, insightful analysis
- Explicit judgements/conclusions that are well supported by evidence/arguments and that include some critical reflection.

parts.content: State **four** specific categories of land or water use that are incorporated into the calculation of an Ecological Footprint (EF).
parts.markscheme:
Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for each of the following up to a maximum of <marks>4 marks</marks>:

1.  **Arable land:** Land required for growing food crops for human consumption or animal feed.
2.  **Pasture land:** Land used for grazing livestock for meat, dairy, or wool production.
3.  **Forest land:** Land required to provide timber products, pulp, and fuel wood.
4.  **Carbon footprint (Carbon uptake land):** The amount of forest land required to sequester the carbon dioxide emissions produced by fossil fuel combustion.
5.  **Built-up land:** Land occupied by infrastructure, including housing, transport, and industrial sites.
6.  **Fishing grounds:** Area of marine and inland water required to support the sustainable harvest of fish and seafood.

parts.content: Outline the essential stages of an Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA) and its role in sustainable decision-making.
parts.markscheme:
### Outline the essential stages and role of an EIA

- **Baseline study:** Conducting an initial survey of the site to establish the current state of the environment (biotic and abiotic factors) before any development begins. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Identification of impacts:** Predicting the potential positive and negative environmental consequences of the proposed project. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Mitigation strategies:** Proposing measures to avoid, reduce, or compensate for the negative impacts identified. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Assessment of alternatives:** Comparing the proposed project with alternative designs or locations to find the least damaging option. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Public consultation:** Involving local communities and stakeholders to ensure social concerns and local knowledge are considered. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Reporting:** Producing a formal Environmental Impact Statement (EIS) for decision-makers to review before granting or denying permission. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Monitoring:** Establishing a plan to track environmental changes during and after construction to ensure mitigation measures are effective. <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award a maximum of <marks>7 marks</marks> for this question.</note>

parts.content: To what extent does the use of the 'Ecological Footprint' (EF) provide an accurate measure of a nation's sustainability?
parts.markscheme:
### Arguments that EF is an accurate/useful measure

- It provides a simple, quantitative model that is easy for the public and policymakers to understand. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- It allows for direct comparison between a population's consumption and the available biocapacity of their region/country. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- It highlights 'ecological overshoot,' where a population's demand exceeds the earth's regenerative capacity. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- It accounts for the indirect land use required for carbon sequestration, linking consumption to climate change. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- It provides a standardized metric that allows for global comparisons between different countries and lifestyles. <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Limitations of EF as an accurate measure

- It is a simplified model that does not account for many types of pollution, such as toxic chemical releases or plastic waste. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- It does not measure the depletion of non-renewable resources (e.g., minerals, ores) until they result in land use or carbon emissions. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- It often ignores the complexity of ecosystem services that do not have a direct 'land area' equivalent (e.g., pollination, water purification). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- It assumes that all land types (e.g., forest vs. pasture) are used at a sustainable yield, which may not be true in practice. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Data quality varies significantly between countries, which can lead to inaccuracies in the calculated footprint. <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award a maximum of <marks>9 marks</marks> for a balanced discussion of both the strengths and limitations of the Ecological Footprint as a model.</note>

questions.specification:
**Table 1: Environmental properties of selected refrigerant compounds**

| Refrigerant Type | Example Compound | Ozone Depletion Potential (ODP) | Global Warming Potential (GWP) | Atmospheric Lifetime (Years) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| CFC (Chlorofluorocarbon) | CFC-12 | 1.00 | 10,900 | 100 |
| HCFC (Hydrochlorofluorocarbon) | HCFC-22 | 0.05 | 1,810 | 12 |
| HFC (Hydrofluorocarbon) | HFC-134a | 0.00 | 1,430 | 14 |
| HFC (Hydrofluorocarbon) | HFC-152a | 0.00 | 124 | 1.4 |

parts.content: State the specific chemical element found in CFCs and HCFCs that is primarily responsible for the catalytic destruction of stratospheric ozone.
parts.markscheme: Chlorine / $$Cl$$ (atoms/radicals) <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: With reference to **Table 1**, describe the trends in Ozone Depletion Potential (ODP) and Global Warming Potential (GWP) as technology transitioned from CFCs to HFCs.
Identify one way in which increased UV radiation reaching the Earth's surface can impact the productivity of terrestrial ecosystems.
Evaluate the use of HFCs as a replacement for CFCs as a strategy for managing global atmospheric pollution.
